skill.name: startup-business-analyst-financial-projections
skill.description: Create detailed 3-5 year financial model with revenue, costs, cash flow, and scenarios
skill.summary: Create detailed 3-5 year financial model with revenue, costs, cash flow, and scenarios
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static backtick findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, fenced formulas, and table examples, not shell execution. The weak cryptography findings are also false positives from ordinary finance or prose terms, with no cryptographic implementation found. The remaining concern is broad declared tool access, including Bash, filesystem write/edit tools, and web tools for a financial projection workflow.

medium_findings.title: Broad tool permissions for financial modeling
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash, Write, Edit, WebSearch, and WebFetch access even though its instructions mainly describe financial projection analysis and report generation. This creates unnecessary execution, filesystem, and network exposure if future prompts or user inputs steer the assistant toward unsafe actions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The broad tool list is directly present in the skill metadata. I did not find malicious instructions, so the concern is excessive capability rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Static external command findings are Markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick locations are inline references, fenced formulas, financial tables, or example prompts. No executable Ruby backtick operation or command substitution instruction was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text shows Markdown examples and tables, not code that runs commands. The skill file is instructional Markdown with no script file or executable block at these locations.

low_findings.title: Static weak cryptography findings are text false positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations do not define or invoke cryptographic algorithms. They are ordinary financial planning or prose content, such as cash flow, modeling references, and design text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain skill description or business planning text, not hash, cipher, or password handling code. No evidence of weak cryptographic use was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Overbroad execution and network capable tool declaration
dangerous_patterns.description: The allowed tool declaration includes Bash and web access. These tools are powerful and should be limited unless a financial projection workflow requires command execution or live web retrieval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The metadata explicitly grants the tools. The risk depends on runtime behavior because the file does not instruct malicious use.

content.value_statement: Early startup teams often need investor-ready projections but lack a structured model. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through revenue, cost, hiring, cash flow, and scenario planning.
actual_capabilities: Gathers business model, pricing, current revenue, cash, growth, cost, and funding assumptions.
Builds cohort-based revenue projections with retention, ARPU, expansion revenue, and growth timing.
Structures expenses across COGS, sales, marketing, research, development, general, and administrative teams.
Creates monthly cash flow views, burn rate estimates, runway calculations, and funding milestones.
Compares conservative, base, and optimistic scenarios using clear assumption changes.
Produces a markdown financial model report with tables, formulas, metrics, and validation checks.
limitations: It does not replace professional accounting, tax, legal, or investment advice.
Projection quality depends on the assumptions and source data supplied by the user.
It creates planning outputs, not native spreadsheet files with live formulas.
Market benchmarks may require current external research before investor use.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Seed Fundraising Model
use_cases.description: Create a three-year projection with ARR, burn, runway, hiring, and funding requirements for investor conversations.

use_cases.title: Build an Operating Budget
use_cases.description: Translate growth targets into department budgets, headcount plans, and cash needs for the next planning cycle.

use_cases.title: Pressure Test Growth Assumptions
use_cases.description: Compare conservative, base, and optimistic scenarios to identify cash risk and milestone sensitivity.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Projection
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a basic 3-year financial projection for my startup. Ask me for the minimum inputs needed before building the model.

prompt_templates.title: Model a SaaS Startup
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a SaaS financial projection using cohort revenue, churn, ARPU, gross margin, sales spend, hiring, burn rate, and runway.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Fundraising Round
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 5-year fundraising model with use of proceeds, hiring milestones, valuation assumptions, cash runway, and key investor metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Run Scenario Sensitivity
prompt_templates.prompt: Build conservative, base, and optimistic scenarios. Show which assumptions most affect runway, ARR, burn multiple, CAC payback, and funding timing.

output_examples.input: B2B SaaS company with $50K MRR, 100 customers, 10 percent monthly churn, and $500K cash.
output_examples.output: A structured projection with revenue cohorts, customer growth, department costs, burn rate, runway, and three scenario summaries.

output_examples.input: Marketplace startup planning a seed round and hiring sales, engineering, and operations roles.
output_examples.output: A funding plan with headcount timing, operating expenses, use of proceeds, milestone targets, and cash balance by quarter.

output_examples.input: Founder wants to know whether 15 percent monthly growth is realistic before meeting investors.
output_examples.output: A sensitivity analysis comparing growth, churn, CAC, pricing, runway, and funding needs across three cases.

best_practices: Provide current revenue, customer count, cash balance, team size, churn, pricing, and cost assumptions.
Review every assumption before sharing projections with investors, board members, or lenders.
Update the model monthly with actual performance and explain major forecast changes.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the projection as audited financial statements or tax advice.
Do not hide weak assumptions by using only an optimistic scenario.
Do not use old market benchmarks without checking current data.

faq.question: What type of businesses can use this skill?
faq.answer: It works best for startups with revenue, customer, cost, hiring, and funding assumptions. SaaS, marketplace, and subscription models fit especially well.

faq.question: Can it create investor-ready projections?
faq.answer: It can create a structured report with common investor metrics. A finance professional should review it before formal use.

faq.question: Does it generate spreadsheet files?
faq.answer: The skill focuses on markdown reports, tables, assumptions, and formulas. Users can transfer the model into Excel or Google Sheets.

faq.question: What inputs should I prepare?
faq.answer: Prepare pricing, current revenue, customer count, churn, acquisition plans, gross margin, team costs, cash balance, and fundraising plans.

faq.question: Can it compare different scenarios?
faq.answer: Yes. It defines conservative, base, and optimistic scenarios using changes to growth, churn, pricing, CAC, and cash needs.

faq.question: Is this financial advice?
faq.answer: No. It is a planning aid for model structure and analysis. It is not accounting, tax, legal, or investment advice.
